pkg_json.name: @fuzdev/fuz_util
pkg_json.logo_alt: a green sauropod wearing a brown utility belt

declarations.name: Args
declarations.docComment:
CLI arguments container.
Positional arguments stored in `_`, named flags/options as string keys.
Produced by `argv_parse` or external parsers (mri, minimist, etc.).

declarations.name: ParsedArgs
declarations.docComment:
Parsed CLI arguments with guaranteed positionals array.
Returned by `argv_parse` which always initializes `_`.

declarations.name: ArgValue
declarations.docComment: Value types supported in CLI arguments.

declarations.name: ArgSchema
declarations.docComment:
Schema description for help text generation.
Not used by `args_parse`/`args_serialize` directly - provided for consumers
building CLI help output.

declarations.name: ArgsAliasesResult
declarations.docComment:
Result of alias extraction from a schema.
Includes canonical keys for downstream conflict detection.

declarations.name: args_validate_schema
declarations.docComment:
Validates a zod schema for CLI arg usage.

Checks for:
- Alias conflicts with canonical keys
- Duplicate aliases across different keys

Results are cached per schema (WeakMap). Safe to call multiple times.
declarations.typeSignature: (schema: ZodType<unknown, unknown, $ZodTypeInternals<unknown, unknown>>): { success: true; } | { success: false; error: ZodError<unknown>; }

parameters.name: schema
parameters.description: zod object schema with optional alias metadata

declarations.returnType: { success: true; } | { success: false; error: ZodError<unknown>; }
declarations.returnDescription: validation result with success flag and optional error

declarations.name: args_parse
declarations.docComment:
Validates parsed CLI args against a zod schema.

Handles CLI-specific concerns before validation:
1. Validates schema (cached) - returns error if alias conflicts exist
2. Expands aliases defined in schema `.meta({aliases: ['v']})`
3. Strips alias keys (required for strictObject schemas)
4. Validates with zod
5. After validation, syncs `no-` prefixed boolean flags with their base keys

Schema analysis is cached per schema (WeakMap) for performance.
declarations.typeSignature: <TOutput extends Record<string, ArgValue> = Args>(unparsed_args: Args, schema: ZodType<TOutput, unknown, $ZodTypeInternals<TOutput, unknown>>): ZodSafeParseResult<...>

parameters.name: unparsed_args
parameters.description: args object from CLI parser (mri, minimist, etc.)

parameters.name: schema
parameters.description: zod object schema with optional alias metadata

declarations.returnDescription: `z.ZodSafeParseResult` with expanded/synced data on success

declarations.name: args_serialize
declarations.docComment:
Serializes Args to CLI string array for subprocess forwarding.

Handles CLI conventions:
- Positionals first, then flags
- Single-char keys get single dash, multi-char get double dash
- Boolean `true` becomes bare flag, `false` is skipped
- `undefined` values are skipped
- `no-` prefixed keys skipped when base key is truthy (avoid contradiction)
- When schema provided, extracts aliases and prefers shortest form

Schema analysis is cached per schema (WeakMap) for performance.
declarations.typeSignature: (args: Args, schema?: ZodType<unknown, unknown, $ZodTypeInternals<unknown, unknown>> | undefined): string[]

parameters.name: schema
parameters.description: optional zod schema to extract aliases for short form preference

declarations.name: args_extract_aliases
declarations.docComment:
Extracts alias mappings and canonical keys from a zod schema's metadata.

Useful for consumers building custom tooling (help generators, conflict detection, etc.).
Results are cached per schema (WeakMap).

Note: Returns copies of the cached data to prevent mutation of internal cache.

parameters.name: schema
parameters.description: zod object schema with optional `.meta({aliases})` on fields

declarations.name: argv_parse
declarations.docComment:
Parses raw CLI argv array into an Args object.

A lightweight, dependency-free alternative to mri/minimist with compatible behavior.

Features:
- `--flag` → `{flag: true}`
- `--flag value` → `{flag: 'value'}` or `{flag: 123}` (numeric coercion)
- `--flag=value` → equals syntax
- `--flag=` → `{flag: ''}` (empty string, differs from mri which returns true)
- `-f` → `{f: true}` (short flag)
- `-f value` → `{f: 'value'}`
- `-abc` → `{a: true, b: true, c: true}` (combined short flags)
- `-abc value` → `{a: true, b: true, c: 'value'}` (last flag gets value)
- `--no-flag` → `{flag: false}` (negation prefix)
- `--` → stops flag parsing, rest become positionals
- Positionals collected in `_` array
- Repeated flags become arrays

Intentional differences from mri:
- `--flag=` returns `''` (mri returns `true`)
- `--flag= next` returns `{flag: '', _: ['next']}` (mri takes `next` as the value)
- `---flag` returns `{'-flag': true}` (mri strips all dashes)
- `['--flag', '']` preserves `''` (mri coerces to `0`)
- `--__proto__` works as a normal key (mri silently fails)

The returned object uses `Object.create(null)` to prevent prototype pollution
and allow any key name including `__proto__` and `constructor`.

parameters.name: argv
parameters.description: raw argument array (typically process.argv.slice(2))

declarations.returnDescription: parsed `Args` object with guaranteed `_` array (null prototype)

declarations.name: to_array
declarations.docComment: Converts `value` to an array if it's not already.
declarations.typeSignature: <T>(value: T): T extends readonly any[] ? T : T[]
declarations.returnType: T extends readonly any[] ? T : T[]

declarations.name: remove_unordered
declarations.docComment: Removes an element from `array` at `index` in an unordered manner.

mutates.array: swaps element at index with last element, then pops

declarations.name: to_next
declarations.docComment:
Returns a function that returns the next item in the `array`
in a linear sequence, looping back to index 0 when it reaches the end.

declarations.name: wait
declarations.docComment: Waits for the given `duration` before resolving.

declarations.name: is_promise
declarations.docComment: Checks if `value` is a `Promise` (or thenable).

declarations.name: Deferred
declarations.docComment: A deferred object with a promise and its resolve/reject handlers.

declarations.name: create_deferred
declarations.docComment: Creates an object with a `promise` and its `resolve`/`reject` handlers.

declarations.name: each_concurrent
declarations.docComment:
Runs a function on each item with controlled concurrency.
Like `map_concurrent` but doesn't collect results (more efficient for side effects).
declarations.typeSignature: <T>(items: Iterable<T>, concurrency: number, fn: (item: T, index: number) => void | Promise<void>, signal?: AbortSignal | undefined): Promise<...>
examples:
```ts
await each_concurrent(
  file_paths,
  5, // max 5 concurrent deletions
  async (path) => { await unlink(path); },
);
```

throws.description: if `concurrency < 1`

parameters.name: concurrency
parameters.description: maximum number of concurrent operations

parameters.name: signal
parameters.description: optional `AbortSignal` to cancel processing

declarations.name: map_concurrent
declarations.docComment: Maps over items with controlled concurrency, preserving input order.
declarations.typeSignature: <T, R>(items: Iterable<T>, concurrency: number, fn: (item: T, index: number) => R | Promise<R>, signal?: AbortSignal | undefined): Promise<...>

throws.description: if `concurrency < 1`

parameters.name: concurrency
parameters.description: maximum number of concurrent operations

parameters.name: signal
parameters.description: optional `AbortSignal` to cancel processing

declarations.returnDescription: array of results in same order as input

declarations.name: map_concurrent_settled
declarations.docComment:
Like `map_concurrent` but collects all results/errors instead of failing fast.
Returns an array of settlement objects matching the `Promise.allSettled` pattern.

On abort, resolves with partial results: completed items keep their real settlements,
in-flight and un-started items are settled as rejected with the abort reason.
declarations.typeSignature: <T, R>(items: Iterable<T>, concurrency: number, fn: (item: T, index: number) => R | Promise<R>, signal?: AbortSignal | undefined): Promise<...>

throws.description: if `concurrency < 1`

parameters.name: concurrency
parameters.description: maximum number of concurrent operations

parameters.name: signal
parameters.description: optional `AbortSignal` to cancel processing

declarations.returnDescription: array of `PromiseSettledResult` objects in input order

declarations.name: AsyncSemaphore
declarations.docComment:
Async semaphore for concurrency limiting.

With `Infinity` permits, `acquire()` always resolves immediately.

throws.description: if `permits < 0`

declarations.name: BenchmarkBaselineEntry
declarations.docComment:
Schema for a single benchmark entry in the baseline.

`outlier_ratio` is persisted as a noise signal independent of the
post-cleaning `std_dev_ns`/cv: outlier removal *deflates* std_dev (the
cleaned set is by construction less variable than the raw set), so cv
alone misses runs where a third of the iterations were tail events.
`benchmark_baseline_compare` OR-gates `noise_warning` on this field so
a noisy raw distribution gets flagged even when the cleaned cv looks tight.
declarations.typeSignature: ZodObject<{ name: ZodString; mean_ns: ZodNumber; p50_ns: ZodNumber; std_dev_ns: ZodNumber; min_ns: ZodNumber; max_ns: ZodNumber; ... 7 more ...; budget: ZodObject<...>; }, $strip>

declarations.name: BenchmarkBaseline
declarations.docComment:
Schema for the complete baseline file.

`metadata` is an opt-in passthrough bag for context that applies to the whole
run but isn't part of the comparison math — corpus identity (file counts,
total bytes), dependency versions, binary sizes, hardware notes, build flags.
Pass it on save and read it back on load; it's not interpreted by
`benchmark_baseline_compare`. The intended use is to let consumers attach
"did this run measure the same thing as the baseline?" context (e.g. corpus
shape) without forking the schema, then surface mismatches in their own
reporting. fuz_util doesn't surface metadata in comparison output because it
has no shape — consumers know what their metadata means and how to display
the diff.
declarations.typeSignature: ZodObject<{ version: ZodNumber; timestamp: ZodString; git_commit: ZodNullable<ZodString>; git_branch: ZodNullable<ZodString>; node_version: ZodString; entries: ZodArray<...>; metadata: ZodOptional<...>; }, $strip>

declarations.name: BenchmarkBaselineSaveOptions
declarations.docComment: Options for saving a baseline.

members.name: path
members.docComment: Directory to store baselines (default: '.gro/benchmarks')

members.name: git_commit
members.docComment: Git commit hash (auto-detected if not provided)

members.name: git_branch
members.docComment: Git branch name (auto-detected if not provided)

members.name: metadata
members.docComment:
Opt-in passthrough for run-level context (corpus identity, dependency
versions, binary sizes, hardware notes). Round-trips on `_load` and is
accessible via `BenchmarkBaselineComparisonResult.baseline_metadata`, but
is *not* interpreted by `_compare` — consumers decide what to do with
mismatches. Keep values JSON-serializable; this is written verbatim to
the baseline file.

declarations.name: BenchmarkBaselineLoadOptions
declarations.docComment: Options for loading a baseline.

members.name: path
members.docComment: Directory to load baseline from (default: '.gro/benchmarks')

declarations.name: BenchmarkBaselineCompareOptions
declarations.docComment: Options for comparing against a baseline.

members.name: regression_threshold
members.docComment:
Minimum speedup ratio to consider a regression.
For example, 1.05 means only flag regressions that are 5% or more slower.
Default: 1.0 (any statistically significant slowdown is a regression)

members.name: staleness_warning_days
members.docComment:
Number of days after which to warn about stale baseline.
Default: undefined (no staleness warning)

members.name: min_percent_difference
members.docComment:
Minimum percentage difference to consider meaningful, as a ratio.
Passed through to `benchmark_stats_compare`. See `BenchmarkCompareOptions`.
Default: 0.10 (10%)

members.name: noise_warning_cv_threshold
members.docComment:
Coefficient of variation (`std_dev / mean`) at or above which a
comparison is flagged with `noise_warning: true`. Doesn't affect
bucketing — the task still routes to regressions / improvements /
unchanged as the Welch math dictates — but tells the formatter (and
any custom consumer) that the underlying measurement is noisy enough
that a "significant" call should be read with skepticism. The default
is calibrated for system-noise/thermal/background-load contamination,
not microbenchmark floor effects; lower it (e.g. 0.15) for
sub-microsecond functions where any cv signal matters, raise it for
inherently noisy workloads where 0.3 fires on every run.
Default: 0.30

members.name: noise_warning_outlier_ratio_threshold
members.docComment:
Outlier ratio (fraction of iterations rejected by MAD outlier removal)
at or above which the comparison is flagged with `noise_warning: true`,
OR-gated with the cv check. Catches the case where the post-cleaning
cv looks tight because outlier removal already deflated the spread —
a third of the iterations being tail events is itself a noise signal,
even if the surviving samples cluster cleanly. Set higher (e.g. 0.2)
for benchmarks where outliers are expected (allocator-bound, async
I/O), lower (e.g. 0.05) for tight CPU loops.
Default: 0.10

declarations.name: BenchmarkBaselineComparisonResult
declarations.docComment: Result of comparing current results against a baseline.

members.name: baseline_found
members.docComment: Whether a baseline was found

members.name: baseline_timestamp
members.docComment: Timestamp of the baseline

members.name: baseline_commit
members.docComment: Git commit of the baseline

members.name: baseline_age_days
members.docComment: Age of the baseline in days

members.name: baseline_stale
members.docComment: Whether the baseline is considered stale based on staleness_warning_days option

members.name: baseline_node_version
members.docComment: Node.js version recorded in the baseline (null if no baseline).

members.name: current_node_version
members.docComment: Node.js version of the process that produced the current results.

members.name: node_version_changed
members.docComment:
True if the baseline's node version differs from the current process.
Informational only — surfaced in the comparison header so readers can
apply the caveat across all task comparisons; does not affect per-task
classification (Node bumps affect every task uniformly).

members.name: baseline_metadata
members.docComment:
The `metadata` bag persisted in the baseline file, or `null` if the
baseline didn't have one (or no baseline was found). Returned verbatim —
fuz_util doesn't validate the shape or diff it against any "current run"
metadata. If the consumer needs a diff, they pass `options.metadata`
(current-run context) and walk the two records themselves.

members.name: comparisons
members.docComment:
Individual task comparisons for tasks where Welch's math is meaningful —
i.e., budget unchanged between baseline and current. Methodology-changed
tasks are NOT in here; they live in `methodology_changed`. Excluding them
here keeps aggregate reads of `c.comparison.faster` /
`c.comparison.speedup_ratio` / `c.comparison.recommendation` safe — those
fields are still computed for methodology-changed rows but answer a
counterfactual question (the math is correct over mismatched sample
sizes) and would mislead consumers iterating this array.

members.name: regressions
members.docComment: Tasks that regressed (slower with statistical significance), sorted by effect size (largest first)

members.name: improvements
members.docComment: Tasks that improved (faster with statistical significance), sorted by effect size (largest first)

members.name: unchanged
members.docComment: Tasks with no significant change

members.name: methodology_changed
members.docComment:
Tasks whose effective budget differs between baseline and current.
Excluded from regressions/improvements/unchanged because the Welch
comparison is contaminated by sample-size or warmup differences — the
math is correct but answers a different question than the reader thinks.
Re-save the baseline after intentional methodology changes to surface
any genuine drift that was masked.

members.name: new_tasks
members.docComment: Tasks in current run but not in baseline

members.name: removed_tasks
members.docComment: Tasks in baseline but not in current run

declarations.name: BenchmarkBaselineTaskComparison
declarations.docComment: Comparison result for a single task.

members.name: comparison
members.docComment:
Welch comparison of baseline vs. current means.

When the row is in `methodology_changed`, **every field** of this object
is contaminated by sample-size or warmup differences — not just
`recommendation`. The Welch math runs end-to-end and populates `faster`,
`speedup_ratio`, `significant`, `p_value`, `percent_difference`,
`effect_size`, `effect_magnitude`, `ci_overlap`, and `recommendation`,
but it's comparing distributions produced under different methodologies.
Treat the result as diagnostic ("how dramatic is the contamination?"),
not authoritative.

The built-in formatters (`benchmark_baseline_format`,
`benchmark_baseline_format_json`) never render `comparison.*` for
methodology-changed rows for this reason — they show only the budget
diff. Custom consumers reading this field directly should check
`methodology_changed` first.

members.name: methodology_changed
members.docComment:
True if the effective time budget differs between baseline and current.
When set, the task is routed to `methodology_changed` instead of
regressions/improvements/unchanged, and the `comparison` field's contents
become unreliable per the doc above.

members.name: noise_warning
members.docComment:
True when measurement noise is high enough to undermine the
significance call on this row. OR-gated across two signals:
  - `max(baseline.cv, current.cv) >= noise_warning_cv_threshold`
    where cv = `std_dev_ns / mean_ns` (post-outlier-removal).
  - `max(baseline.outlier_ratio, current.outlier_ratio)
    >= noise_warning_outlier_ratio_threshold`, since outlier removal
    deflates cv — a high outlier ratio is itself a noise signal even
    when the cleaned cv looks tight.
Independent of `methodology_changed`. The Welch math still ran; this
flag tells the reader to take `comparison.significant` with skepticism
on this row. Cv is recomputed at comparison time from persisted
`mean_ns`/`std_dev_ns`; `outlier_ratio` is persisted directly.

members.name: max_cv
members.docComment:
The larger of the two coefficients of variation across baseline and
current, exposed so consumers can render the actual noise level.
Recomputed at comparison time from persisted `mean_ns` and
`std_dev_ns` — not a persisted field.

members.name: max_outlier_ratio
members.docComment:
The larger of the two outlier ratios across baseline and current.
Read directly from the persisted entries. Exposed so consumers can
render the actual outlier rate alongside `noise_warning`.

declarations.name: BenchmarkBudgetDiffEntry
declarations.docComment:
Per-field diff entry produced by `benchmark_budget_diff`. The `async_resolved`
entry carries booleans; every other entry carries numbers. Consumers reading
`baseline`/`current` should narrow on `field` before using the value
arithmetically.

declarations.name: benchmark_budget_diff
declarations.docComment:
Compute the per-field diff between two effective budgets. Returns an empty
array if budgets are identical — the formatters use the length as a quick
"methodology changed?" check without re-deriving the boolean.
declarations.typeSignature: (baseline: { duration_ms: number; warmup_iterations: number; min_iterations: number; max_iterations: number; async_resolved: boolean; }, current: { duration_ms: number; warmup_iterations: number; min_iterations: number; max_iterations: number; async_resolved: boolean; }): BenchmarkBudgetDiffEntry[]

parameters.name: baseline
parameters.type: { duration_ms: number; warmup_iterations: number; min_iterations: number; max_iterations: number; async_resolved: boolean; }

parameters.name: current
parameters.type: { duration_ms: number; warmup_iterations: number; min_iterations: number; max_iterations: number; async_resolved: boolean; }

declarations.name: benchmark_baseline_save
declarations.docComment:
Save benchmark results as the current baseline.

Each entry's effective time-budget (`duration_ms`, `warmup_iterations`,
`min_iterations`, `max_iterations`) is persisted alongside the stats so a
later `benchmark_baseline_compare` can detect methodology drift — a budget
mismatch between baseline and current routes the task to the
`methodology_changed` bucket instead of producing false regressions /
improvements. Suite-level config (`timer`, `cooldown_ms`) is *not*
persisted; keep it stable across runs in the same baseline lineage.

throws.description: if creating the baseline directory or writing the file fails

declarations.name: benchmark_baseline_load
declarations.docComment: Load the current baseline from disk.
declarations.typeSignature: (options?: BenchmarkBaselineLoadOptions): Promise<{ version: number; timestamp: string; git_commit: string | null; git_branch: string | null; node_version: string; entries: { ...; }[]; metadata?: Record<...> | undefined; } | null>
declarations.returnType: Promise<{ version: number; timestamp: string; git_commit: string | null; git_branch: string | null; node_version: string; entries: { name: string; mean_ns: number; p50_ns: number; std_dev_ns: number; ... 9 more ...; budget: { ...; }; }[]; metadata?: Record<...> | undefined; } | null>
declarations.returnDescription: the baseline, or null if not found or invalid

declarations.name: benchmark_baseline_compare
declarations.docComment: Compare benchmark results against the stored baseline.

parameters.name: options
parameters.description: comparison options including regression threshold and staleness warning

declarations.returnDescription: comparison result with regressions, improvements, and unchanged tasks

declarations.name: benchmark_baseline_format
declarations.docComment: Format a baseline comparison result as a human-readable string.

parameters.name: result
parameters.description: comparison result from `benchmark_baseline_compare`

declarations.name: benchmark_baseline_format_json
declarations.docComment: Format a baseline comparison result as JSON for programmatic consumption.
declarations.typeSignature: (result: BenchmarkBaselineComparisonResult, options?: { pretty?: boolean | undefined; }): string

parameters.name: result
parameters.description: comparison result from `benchmark_baseline_compare`

modules.moduleComment:
Benchmark baseline storage and comparison utilities.
Save benchmark results to disk and compare against baselines for regression detection.

declarations.name: benchmark_format_table
declarations.docComment:
Format results as an ASCII table with percentiles, min/max, and relative performance.
All times use the same unit for easy comparison.

throws.description: if `baseline` is provided but no result has that name

parameters.name: baseline
parameters.description: optional task name to use as baseline for comparison (defaults to fastest)

declarations.name: benchmark_format_markdown
declarations.docComment:
Format results as a Markdown table with key metrics.
All times use the same unit for easy comparison.

throws.description: if `baseline` is provided but no result has that name

parameters.name: baseline
parameters.description: optional task name to use as baseline for comparison (defaults to fastest)

declarations.name: benchmark_format_markdown_grouped
declarations.docComment: Format results as grouped Markdown tables with headers between groups.

members.name: pretty
members.docComment: Whether to pretty-print (default: true)

members.name: include_timings
members.docComment: Whether to include raw timings array (default: false, can be large)

declarations.name: benchmark_format_json
declarations.docComment: Format results as JSON.

declarations.name: benchmark_format_table_grouped
declarations.docComment: Format results as a grouped table with visual separators between groups.

declarations.name: benchmark_format_number
declarations.docComment: Format a number with fixed decimal places and thousands separators.

declarations.name: BenchmarkStatsComparable
declarations.docComment:
Minimal stats interface for comparison.
This allows comparing stats from different sources (e.g., loaded baselines).

declarations.name: EffectMagnitude
declarations.docComment: Effect size magnitude interpretation (Cohen's d).

declarations.name: BenchmarkComparison
declarations.docComment: Result from comparing two benchmark stats.

members.name: faster
members.docComment: Which benchmark is faster ('a', 'b', or 'equal' if difference is negligible)

members.name: speedup_ratio
members.docComment: How much faster the winner is (e.g., 1.5 means 1.5x faster)

members.name: significant
members.docComment: Whether the difference is both statistically and practically significant

members.name: p_value
members.docComment: P-value from Welch's t-test (lower = more confident the difference is real)

members.name: percent_difference
members.docComment: Percentage difference between means as a ratio (0.05 = 5%, 1.0 = 100%)

members.name: effect_size
members.docComment: Cohen's d effect size (informational — not used for classification)

members.name: effect_magnitude
members.docComment: Interpretation of practical significance based on percentage difference

members.name: ci_overlap
members.docComment:
Whether the 95% confidence intervals of the two means overlap.

**Informational only — do not use this field to infer significance.**
Two means with overlapping 95% CIs can still differ significantly at
p<0.05 (overlap of up to ~25% of CI width is consistent with
significance); conversely, non-overlapping CIs are slightly *stronger*
than the standard p<0.05 bar but the relationship is not strict.
Use `significant` and `p_value` for classification. This field is
exposed for consumers who want to render CIs side-by-side and need
the visual overlap check.

Note: the underlying CIs are computed with a z-score (1.96) rather
than the strict t-score, so at small n the CIs are slightly narrow
— overlap is reported less often than a t-based CI would. Effect is
~2-3% at the n=30 floor after Bessel's correction on `std_dev_ns`.

members.name: recommendation
members.docComment: Human-readable interpretation of the comparison

declarations.name: BenchmarkCompareOptions
declarations.docComment: Options for benchmark comparison.

members.name: alpha
members.docComment: Significance level for hypothesis testing (default: 0.05)

members.name: min_percent_difference
members.docComment:
Minimum percentage difference to consider practically meaningful, as a ratio.
Below this threshold, differences are classified as 'negligible' and
`significant` is forced to `false`, regardless of p-value.
This prevents the t-test's oversensitivity at large sample sizes from
flagging system-level noise (thermal throttle, OS scheduler, cache pressure)
as meaningful differences.

Effect magnitude thresholds scale from this value:
negligible < min, small < min*3, medium < min*5, large >= min*5.

Default: 0.10 (10%).

declarations.name: BenchmarkStats
declarations.docComment:
Complete statistical analysis of timing measurements.
Includes outlier detection, descriptive statistics, and performance metrics.
All timing values are in nanoseconds.

**Outliers are treated asymmetrically**, because high and low outliers in
timing data mean different things:

- Upper-tail order statistics (`max_ns`, `p75_ns`–`p99_ns`) are computed over
  the **raw** valid timings — high outliers (GC pauses, slow paths) are real
  latency events, so the tail stays honest and p99 reflects real events
  rather than a pre-stripped distribution.
- `min_ns` is computed over the **MAD-cleaned** timings — nothing runs faster
  than its true cost, so a low outlier is an invalid measurement, not a fast
  run, and reporting it as the best case would mislead.
- Central-tendency statistics (`mean_ns`, `std_dev_ns`, `cv`,
  `confidence_interval_ns`, `ops_per_second`) are computed over the
  **MAD-cleaned** timings so the Welch's-t comparison keeps a stable mean.

`p50_ns` (median) uses raw timings to stay paired with the percentile family;
being robust, it's unaffected in practice. `outlier_ratio` reports how heavy
the tail was either way. `sample_size` is the cleaned count behind central
tendency and `min_ns`; the upper-tail order statistics use all valid timings
(`sample_size` + `outliers_ns.length`); `raw_sample_size` is the total input
count including the `failed_iterations` invalid values that were filtered out.

members.name: mean_ns
members.docComment: Mean (average) time in nanoseconds (over MAD-cleaned samples)

members.name: p50_ns
members.docComment: 50th percentile (median) time in nanoseconds (over raw samples)

members.name: std_dev_ns
members.docComment: Standard deviation in nanoseconds (over MAD-cleaned samples)

members.name: min_ns

declarations.name: benchmark_stats_compare
declarations.docComment:
Compare two benchmark results for practical and statistical significance.
Uses percentage difference for effect magnitude classification, with Welch's
t-test for statistical confidence. Cohen's d is computed as an informational
metric but does not drive classification — its thresholds (0.2/0.5/0.8) are
calibrated for social science and produce false positives in benchmarking
where within-run variance is tight.
declarations.typeSignature: (a: BenchmarkStatsComparable, b: BenchmarkStatsComparable, options?: BenchmarkCompareOptions | undefined): BenchmarkComparison

parameters.name: a
parameters.description: first benchmark stats (or any object with required properties)

parameters.name: b
parameters.description: second benchmark stats (or any object with required properties)

declarations.returnDescription: comparison result with significance, effect size, and recommendation

modules.moduleComment:
Benchmark-specific statistical analysis.
Uses the general stats utilities from `stats.ts` for timing/performance analysis.
All timing values are in nanoseconds.

declarations.name: BenchmarkConfig
declarations.docComment: Configuration options for a benchmark suite.

members.name: duration_ms
members.docComment:
Target measurement duration per task in milliseconds. The loop runs
at least `min_iterations` iterations *and* at least this long, with
`max_iterations` and `on_iteration` `abort()` as hard ceilings.
Default: 1000ms

members.name: warmup_iterations
members.docComment:
Number of warmup iterations before actual measurements.
Warmup helps stabilize JIT compilation and caches.
Default: 10

members.name: cooldown_ms
members.docComment:
Cooldown time between tasks in milliseconds. Lets the runtime settle
GC and (partially) thermal state between tasks. Fixed regardless of
the previous task's duration or allocation footprint — if a suite
mixes heavy and light tasks, raise this so light tasks downstream
don't run in the prior task's GC shadow.
Default: 100ms

members.name: min_iterations
members.docComment:
Minimum number of iterations to run. The loop continues past
`duration_ms` if needed to reach this floor, so raising it in a slow
suite extends wall-clock past `duration_ms`.

The default (30) is sized so the Welch's-t DOF approximation used by
`benchmark_stats_compare` stays stable on the floor case. Lowering it
below ~15 produces statistically unreliable significance calls on slow
tasks that hit the floor exactly — the math still runs but
`comparison.significant` becomes a coin flip on noisy outliers.

Default: 30

members.name: max_iterations
members.docComment:
Maximum number of iterations to run. Prevents infinite loops on
extremely fast functions, and also sizes the pre-allocated timings
array — set this only as high as you expect to fill, since oversized
caps waste memory and add GC pressure during measurement.
Default: 100000

members.name: timer
members.docComment:
Custom timer to use for measurements.
Default: `timer_default` (auto-detects environment)

members.name: on_iteration
members.docComment:
Callback invoked after each iteration completes.
Useful for triggering garbage collection, logging progress, early termination,
or custom instrumentation.

**Note**: The callback time is NOT included in iteration measurements - it runs
after the timing capture. However, frequent GC calls will slow overall benchmark
execution time.
members.typeSignature: (task_name: string, iteration: number, abort: () => void) => void

members.name: on_task_complete
members.docComment:
Callback invoked after each task completes.
Useful for logging progress during long benchmark runs.
members.typeSignature: (result: BenchmarkResult, index: number, total: number) => void

declarations.name: BenchmarkTask
declarations.docComment:
A benchmark task to execute.

The time-budget fields (`duration_ms`, `warmup_iterations`, `min_iterations`,
`max_iterations`) override the suite-level `BenchmarkConfig` for this task
only. Use them when one task is much faster or slower than the others —
e.g. raise `min_iterations` on a slow task so its percentile/CI math has
enough samples without inflating the budget for the fast tasks.

**Reading output across overrides:** when per-task overrides diverge
across rows in the same table, cross-row comparison weakens. Percentiles
(`p50`, `p99`, …) become unreliable when sample counts differ — `p99` at
n=30 and `p99` at n=50000 estimate different things. Means (and the
`vs Best` column) stay reasonable only if `warmup_iterations` is held
comparable across tasks; asymmetric warmup biases the under-warmed task's
mean upward. Per-task percentiles remain valid for that task alone.

members.name: name
members.docComment: Name of the task (for display)

members.name: fn
members.docComment: Function to benchmark (sync or async). Return values are ignored.

members.name: setup
members.docComment:
Optional setup function run before benchmarking this task.
Not included in timing measurements.

Mutations to `fn` and `name` made here are honored by the measurement
loop — useful for dynamic configuration that depends on async state
resolved during setup.

members.name: teardown
members.docComment:
Optional teardown function run after benchmarking this task.
Not included in timing measurements.

members.name: skip
members.docComment:
If true, skip this task during benchmark runs.
Useful for temporarily disabling tasks during development.

members.name: only
members.docComment:
If true, run only this task (and other tasks marked `only`).
Useful for focusing on specific tasks during development.

members.name: async
members.docComment:
Hint for whether the function is sync or async. Auto-detected during
warmup if not set; `async: false` skips per-iteration promise checking
for sync functions, while `async: true` forces an `await` on every
measurement iteration.

Setting `async: true` on a function that returns sync forces an
unnecessary microtask per iteration — for sub-microsecond functions
this adds measurable bias. Prefer leaving `async` undefined (auto-
detected during warmup) unless the conditional-async hazard below
applies.

**Required for conditional-async fns** — without `async: true`, a
first call that happens to return synchronously locks in the sync
code path and any later Promise returns leak as unhandled rejections.

members.name: duration_ms
members.docComment:
Override the suite's `duration_ms` for this task only.
Useful when one task is much slower than others and needs a longer (or shorter)
measurement window than the suite default.

members.name: warmup_iterations
min_iterations
members.docComment:
Override the suite's `min_iterations` for this task only.
Raise this on slow tasks so percentile and CI math have enough
samples. Wins over `duration_ms` (see the suite field) — a slow task
with a raised floor extends wall-clock until the count is reached.

Prefer raising this over `duration_ms` when you need more samples:
per-iteration noise (GC, scheduler, thermal) is a time-rate process,
so a longer wall-clock window proportionally inflates exposure to
rare tail events. Raising the sample floor fixes the statistical-power
problem without that inflation.

members.name: max_iterations
members.docComment:
Override the suite's `max_iterations` for this task only.
Cap a fast task to a fixed sample count, or raise the ceiling on a
slow task that would otherwise be limited by the suite default. Also
sizes the per-task pre-allocation — avoid extreme caps that won't
actually be filled. When this differs sharply across tasks in the
same suite, the larger allocation can leak GC pressure into
subsequent tasks (`cooldown_ms` is fixed regardless of prior task
footprint).

declarations.name: BenchmarkBudget
declarations.docComment:
Effective time-budget config used to produce a benchmark sample set.
Resolved from per-task overrides on top of suite defaults — what the
measurement loop actually saw, not what was configured.

`async_resolved` captures the boolean that `benchmark_warmup` actually
returned for the measurement run — *not* the user's `task.async` hint.
The two diverge when (a) the hint is `undefined` and the function is
auto-detected sync vs. async, or (b) a conditional-async fn resolves
differently between runs. Persisting the resolved value is what makes
the budget describe "what the loop saw," not "what was configured."

declarations.name: BenchmarkResult
declarations.docComment: Result from running a single benchmark task.

members.name: name
members.docComment: Task name

members.name: stats
members.docComment: Statistical analysis of the benchmark

members.name: iterations
members.docComment: Number of iterations executed

members.name: total_time_ms
members.docComment: Total wall-clock time for the task (setup + warmup + measurement + teardown) in milliseconds

members.name: timings_ns
members.docComment:
Raw timing data for each iteration in nanoseconds.
Length equals `iterations` (the array is right-sized after measurement).
Useful for custom statistical analysis, histogram generation,
or exporting to external tools.

members.name: budget
members.docComment:
Effective per-task time budget after applying overrides to suite defaults.
Persisted into baselines so `benchmark_baseline_compare` can detect
methodology drift — a `min_iterations` bump between baseline and current
shifts Welch's DOF and produces "regressions" that are sample-size
artifacts, not real drift.

declarations.name: BenchmarkFormatTableOptions
declarations.docComment: Options for table formatting.

members.name: groups
members.docComment: Group results by category using filter functions.

declarations.name: BenchmarkGroup
declarations.docComment: A group definition for organizing benchmark results.

members.name: name
members.docComment: Display name for the group

members.name: description
members.docComment: Optional description shown below the group name

members.name: filter
members.docComment: Filter function to determine which results belong to this group

members.name: baseline
members.docComment:
Task name to use as baseline for the "vs" column.
When specified, ratios are computed against this task instead of the fastest.
If the baseline task is not found in the group, falls back to "vs Best" with a warning.

declarations.name: benchmark_warmup
declarations.docComment:
Warmup function by running it multiple times.
Detects whether the function is async based on return value.

When no `async_hint` is provided, at least one detection iteration runs even
if `iterations` is 0 — otherwise async detection would be impossible and
async functions would have their returned promises leaked as unhandled.
declarations.typeSignature: (fn: () => unknown, iterations: number, async_hint?: boolean | undefined): Promise<boolean>

parameters.name: fn
parameters.description: function to warmup (sync or async)

parameters.name: async_hint
parameters.description: if provided, use this instead of detecting

declarations.returnDescription: whether the function is async

declarations.name: Benchmark
declarations.docComment: Benchmark class for measuring and comparing function performance.

members.name: add
members.docComment: Add a benchmark task.

throws.description: if a task with the same name already exists, or if `fn` is missing when `name` is a string

parameters.name: name
parameters.description: task name or full task object

parameters.name: fn
parameters.description: Function to benchmark (if name is string). Return values are ignored.

parameters.name: name
parameters.description: task name or full task object

parameters.name: fn
parameters.description: Function to benchmark (if name is string). Return values are ignored.

members.name: remove
members.docComment: Remove a benchmark task by name.

throws.description: if task with given name doesn't exist

members.name: run
members.docComment:
Run all benchmark tasks.

Tasks execute in `add()` order. The first task runs against a colder
runtime than subsequent ones (uncompiled JS, cold caches) — a
property of in-process benchmarking that matters more when an early
task has aggressive overrides like low `warmup_iterations` or
`min_iterations`. If first-position bias is a concern, put a
throwaway warm-up task first, or call `run()` twice and use the
second result set.

members.name: table

members.name: markdown

members.docComment: Get the benchmark results.
members.returnDescription: shallow copy of the results array (prevents external mutation)

members.name: results_by_name
members.docComment: Get results as a map for convenient lookup by task name.

Reset the benchmark results.
Keeps tasks intact so benchmarks can be rerun.

members.docComment: Check if the benchmark has been run and has results.

declarations.name: run_dag
declarations.docComment:
Execute nodes in a dependency graph concurrently.

Independent nodes (no unmet dependencies) run in parallel up to
`max_concurrency`. When a node completes, its dependents become
eligible to start. Failure cascading and stop-on-failure are handled
per the options.

parameters.name: options
parameters.description: DAG execution options

declarations.returnDescription: aggregated result with per-node details

modules.moduleComment:
Generic concurrent DAG executor.

Executes nodes in a dependency graph with configurable concurrency,
failure handling, and skip semantics. Nodes without dependency edges
run in parallel (up to max_concurrency). Dependencies are respected
via per-node deferreds.

declarations.name: get_datetime_now
declarations.docComment: Returns an ISO 8601 datetime string for the current time.

modules.moduleComment: Datetime utilities — branded ISO 8601 datetime Zod schema and factory.

declarations.name: deep_equal
declarations.docComment:
Deep equality comparison that checks both structure and type.

Key behaviors:

- Compares by constructor to prevent type confusion (security: `{}` ≠ `[]`, `{}` ≠ `new Map()`, `new ClassA()` ≠ `new ClassB()`)
- Prevents asymmetry bugs: `deep_equal(a, b)` always equals `deep_equal(b, a)`
- Compares only enumerable own properties (ignores prototypes, symbols, non-enumerable)
- Special handling for: Date (timestamp), Number/Boolean (boxed primitives), Error (message/name + enumerable own properties)
- Promises always return false (cannot be meaningfully compared)
- Maps/Sets compare by reference for object keys/values
declarations.returnDescription: true if deeply equal, false otherwise

declarations.name: swallow
declarations.docComment: Stops an event from bubbling and doing default behavior.

Handles the value of an event's target and invokes a callback.
Defaults to swallowing the event to prevent default actions and propagation.
declarations.typeSignature: (cb: (value: any, event: any) => void, swallow_event?: boolean): (e: any) => void

declarations.name: is_iframed

declarations.name: UnreachableError
declarations.docComment:
Error for asserting unreachable code paths in TypeScript.
Useful for exhaustive matching.

members.name: constructor
members.typeSignature: (value: never, message?: string, options?: ErrorOptions | undefined): UnreachableError

declarations.name: unreachable
declarations.docComment:
Beyond terseness, this is useful because `throw` is not an expression,
and therefore can't be used in places like Svelte markup without a workaround,
at least until this proposal is accepted and widely available:
https://github.com/tc39/proposal-throw-expressions
declarations.typeSignature: (value: never, message?: string | undefined): asserts value is never

declarations.name: FACT_HASH_PREFIX
declarations.docComment: Algorithm prefix on every fact hash. The colon is the separator.

declarations.name: FACT_HASH_PATTERN
declarations.docComment:
Pattern for detecting a fact hash anywhere in text.

Has the global flag because the primary use is `String.matchAll` over
cell data / fact bytes. Callers that only need to validate a single
known string should use `is_fact_hash` instead — `RegExp.test` mutates
`lastIndex` on global patterns.

The trailing `(?=blake3:|[^0-9a-f]|$)` lookahead enforces a right
boundary so a 64-hex digest is matched only when it actually *ends*:
followed by a non-hex char, the end of string, or the start of another
`blake3:` ref. This rejects malformed over-long runs (`blake3:` + 65+
hex) rather than silently truncating them to a different valid-shaped
hash, while still matching two refs concatenated with no separator
(the `blake3:` alternative is needed because the prefix itself begins
with the hex char `b`). A bare `(?![0-9a-f])` would instead drop the
first of two glued refs.

declarations.name: FactHashSchema
declarations.docComment:
Wire-form schema for a `blake3:`-prefixed fact hash. Branded so the
type system distinguishes a fact hash from any other `string`,
mirroring `Uuid` (`id.ts`). Construct only via `fact_hash_bytes` /
`fact_hash_stream` / `FactHashSchema.parse(s)` — direct string literals
don't satisfy the brand.

Both client-side (cell payloads) and server-side (DB-row hashes)
consumers reuse this same schema.

declarations.name: fact_hash_bytes
declarations.docComment:
Synchronously hash bytes into a fact hash.

Delegates to `hash_blake3` (the fuz_util wrapper around `@fuzdev/blake3_wasm`)
and prefixes the result with `blake3:`. Strings are UTF-8 encoded.

declarations.name: fact_hash_stream
declarations.docComment:
Hash a `ReadableStream<Uint8Array>` into a fact hash without buffering
the full content. Used by `FactStore.put_ref` for large external content.

declarations.name: is_fact_hash
declarations.docComment:
Type guard. Useful when receiving a hash from an external boundary —
narrows `string` to `FactHash` without going through Zod.

declarations.name: fact_hash_verify
declarations.docComment:
Verify that `bytes` produce the claimed `hash`.

Returns `false` on any mismatch, including a malformed hash. Callers
doing security-sensitive integrity checks should treat `false` as
not-found / corrupt and refuse to use the bytes.

declarations.name: fact_hash_extract_refs
declarations.docComment:
Walk a JSON value collecting every `blake3:`-prefixed string match.

Strings, object values, and array elements are scanned; object keys are
intentionally skipped (a hash as an object key is exotic enough that
callers should declare it explicitly via `FactStore.put({refs})`). The
same hash appearing twice is deduplicated. Order follows depth-first
traversal of the input.

Used by application code on cell snapshot writes and JSON fact writes.

modules.moduleComment:
Fact hash utilities.

A `FactHash` is a `blake3:`-prefixed hex64 blake3 digest. The prefix makes
the hash self-identifying — any text scanner can find it without prior
knowledge of the source.

Branded via Zod (`FactHashSchema`), mirroring `Uuid`. Runtime validation
happens at construction (`fact_hash_bytes` / `fact_hash_stream` cast at
the source; `FactHashSchema.parse` / `is_fact_hash` validate inputs from
external boundaries).

The hash-producing helpers carry the `fact_hash_` prefix so they namespace
cleanly alongside the branded `FactHash` type and the `FACT_HASH_*`
constants, and stay distinct from the raw-hex `hash_blake3` / `hash_sha256`
family in `hash.ts` (those return bare digests; these return the branded,
`blake3:`-prefixed wire form).

declarations.name: FactPutOptions
declarations.docComment:
Optional metadata + ref declarations on a `put` / `put_ref` call.

`content_type` is advisory and not part of the hash. Omitting it
(`undefined`) reads back from `get_meta` as `null` — `undefined` means
"caller didn't specify," `null` means "no type stored." `refs` lets
callers declare references for binary content where text scanning isn't
sound; for JSON content, implementations may auto-extract refs from the
bytes when no explicit `refs` is supplied.

declarations.name: PutStreamOutcome
declarations.docComment:
Outcome of a streaming `put_stream` — the finalized digests + byte count.

`hash` is the `blake3:`-prefixed fact hash (content address); `sha256` is the
bare-hex SHA-256 computed in the same pass, so a consumer can persist it
alongside the fact and let clients verify downloads with the ubiquitous
`sha256sum` (there is no universal shell BLAKE3 tool); `size` is the streamed
byte count. Mirrors the Rust `PutStreamOutcome`.

declarations.name: FactMeta
declarations.docComment:
Per-fact metadata returned by `FactStore.get_meta`.

`external` is `true` when the fact is stored as a URL reference rather
than embedded bytes — callers may use this to decide whether to stream
vs load fully. The external URL itself is intentionally *not* exposed
here: reads go through `get`, which fetches and verifies hash↔bytes
internally, so handing out a live URL would bypass the verifiable-reads
contract. The URL surfaces only from `delete`, where the caller must
unlink the external resource.

declarations.name: FactStore
declarations.docComment:
Backend-agnostic content-addressed byte store.

- **Idempotent writes.** `put(x)` and `put(x)` again — same hash, no
  error, no duplication. Content type and refs from the first write win.
- **Verifiable reads.** Implementations should verify hash↔bytes on
  reads from external storage; embedded reads can skip verify because
  the storage is the hash table.
- **Backend-agnostic.** Composes — a frontline store can check local,
  then remote, then peer (deferred).

members.name: put
members.docComment:
Store bytes, return their hash. Idempotent — storing the same bytes
twice is a no-op that returns the same hash.

members.name: put_stream
members.docComment:
Stream bytes into the store with bounded memory, returning the finalized
digests + size. Hashes BLAKE3 (content address) **and** SHA-256 in a single
pass over the stream, buffers in memory only up to the embedded threshold,
then spills to the disk CAS — so a multi-GB upload never buffers in RAM.

Enforces `max_bytes` mid-stream: a body whose running byte count passes the
cap throws `PayloadTooLargeError` (the backstop for a chunked or
mis-declared `Content-Length`). A disk-full (`ENOSPC`) mid-stream throws
`StorageFullError`. Idempotent like `put` — identical bytes land on the same
content-addressed path and the underlying insert is `ON CONFLICT DO NOTHING`.

The streaming twin of `put`; mirrors the Rust `FactStore::put_stream`.
members.typeSignature:
(
		stream: ReadableStream<Uint8Array>,
		max_bytes: number,
		options?: FactPutOptions,
	) => Promise<PutStreamOutcome>

members.name: put_ref
members.docComment:
Store a reference to external content (large files). Hashes the content
at the URL (streaming) and records hash + URL + size.

`size` is supplied by the caller (typically from the upload's
Content-Length); implementations may verify by counting bytes during
streaming and rejecting on mismatch. Implementations decide how to
fetch — production typically a signed URL into object storage, tests
inject a stub.
members.typeSignature: (url: string, size: number, options?: FactPutOptions) => Promise<FactHash>

members.name: get
members.docComment:
Retrieve bytes by hash. Returns `null` when not found OR when fetching
external content failed integrity verification — both are treated as
unavailable from the caller's perspective.

members.name: has
members.docComment: Existence check. Cheaper than `get` when bytes aren't needed.

members.name: get_meta
members.docComment: Metadata about a stored fact. Returns `null` when not found.

members.name: get_refs
members.docComment:
Declared references for a fact (target hashes only). Returns `[]` for an
absent hash as well as a present-but-ref-less fact — absence-as-no-refs
is what a graph walker wants (a missing fact has no outgoing edges to
follow), and callers needing presence should use `has` / `get_meta`.

members.name: delete
members.docComment:
Drop a fact. Idempotent — deleting an absent hash returns `null`.
Returns the deleted fact's `size` and `external_url` so callers can
tally freed bytes and unlink any external resource (the store
doesn't know how to resolve `file:` / `s3:` / etc. URLs to a
deletable handle, mirroring the read-side fetcher split).

Implementations do NOT verify the hash is unreferenced — that
policy lives one layer up (orphan-fact admin, GC walker).
members.typeSignature: (hash: FactHash) => Promise<{size: number; external_url: string | null} | null>

modules.moduleComment:
`FactStore` interface.

Backend-agnostic content-addressed byte store. Implementations vary by
context (filesystem, PG, federated composition later) but all share this
interface so callers don't care which. The interface lives in fuz_util
(zero backend deps) so build tools can use it; backend implementations
live downstream.

Idempotent writes, verifiable reads, embedded vs referenced storage
abstracted — see the per-method contracts below.

members.name: request
members.docComment: The `request.headers` take precedence over the headers computed from other options.

declarations.name: fetch_value
declarations.docComment:
Specializes `fetch` with some slightly different behavior and additional features:

- throws on ratelimit errors to mitigate unintentional abuse
- optional `parse` function called on the return value
- optional cache (different from the browser cache,
	 the caller can serialize it so e.g. dev setups can avoid hitting the network)
- optional simplified API for authorization and data types
  (you can still provide headers directly)

Unlike `fetch`, this throws on ratelimits (status code 429)
to halt whatever is happening in its tracks to avoid accidental abuse,
but returns a `Result` in all other cases.
Handling ratelimit headers with more sophistication gets tricky because behavior
differs across services.
(e.g. Mastodon returns an ISO string for `x-ratelimit-reset`,
but GitHub returns `Date.now()/1000`,
and other services may do whatever, or even use a different header)

It's also stateless to avoid the complexity and bugs,
so we don't try to track `x-ratelimit-remaining` per domain.

If the `value` is cached, only the cached safe subset of the `headers` are returned.
(currently just `etag` and `last-modified`)
Otherwise the full `res.headers` are included.
declarations.typeSignature: <TValue = any, TParams = undefined>(url: string | URL, options?: FetchValueOptions<TValue, TParams> | undefined): Promise<Result<{ value: TValue; headers: Headers; }, { ...; }>>
declarations.returnType: Promise<Result<{ value: TValue; headers: Headers; }, { status: number; message: string; }>>

declarations.name: FetchValueCacheItem
declarations.typeSignature: ZodObject<{ key: ZodString; url: ZodString; params: ZodAny; value: ZodAny; etag: ZodNullable<ZodString>; last_modified: ZodNullable<ZodString>; }, $strip>

declarations.name: FetchValueCache
declarations.typeSignature: ZodMap<ZodString, ZodObject<{ key: ZodString; url: ZodString; params: ZodAny; value: ZodAny; etag: ZodNullable<ZodString>; last_modified: ZodNullable<...>; }, $strip>>

declarations.name: to_fetch_value_cache_key
declarations.typeSignature: (url: string, params: any, method: string): FetchValueCacheKey

declarations.name: serialize_cache
declarations.docComment: Converts `FetchValueCache` to a JSON string.
declarations.typeSignature: (cache: Map<string, { key: string; url: string; params: any; value: any; etag: string | null; last_modified: string | null; }>): string

parameters.name: cache
parameters.type: Map<string, { key: string; url: string; params: any; value: any; etag: string | null; last_modified: string | null; }>

declarations.name: deserialize_cache
declarations.docComment: Converts a serialized cache string to a `FetchValueCache`.
declarations.typeSignature: (serialized: string): Map<string, { key: string; url: string; params: any; value: any; etag: string | null; last_modified: string | null; }>
declarations.returnType: Map<string, { key: string; url: string; params: any; value: any; etag: string | null; last_modified: string | null; }>

declarations.name: FRACTIONAL_INDEX_ALPHABET
declarations.docComment: Base62 alphabet, monotonic in lex order: digits, then upper, then lower.

declarations.name: FRACTIONAL_INDEX_REGEX
declarations.docComment:
Wire-grammar regex for `cell_item.position`: at least one base62 digit.
The stricter no-trailing-`'0'` rule is an invariant of *this* generator,
not the wire. See module docstring §"Wire vs. emitted invariants".

declarations.name: FRACTIONAL_INDEX_LENGTH_MAX
declarations.docComment:
Soft length cap on positions, mirrored at the wire. Primarily DOS
protection; the algorithm has no inherent length limit but realistic
UX bounds keep emitted keys under 100 chars even after thousands of
consecutive front- or back-inserts. Inputs above this cap surface as
an explicit throw rather than producing oversized output the wire
would later reject.

declarations.name: fractional_index_between
declarations.docComment:
Returns a key strictly between `a` and `b` in lex order.

Either bound may be `null` (open). Throws when the bracket is invalid
(`a >= b` lex-wise) or when the gap is structurally too tight to fit
a key under the no-trailing-`'0'` invariant (only happens with bounds
shaped `(a, a + '0…0')`; the helper itself never produces those).

Emits a deterministic mid-key, then appends a random jitter suffix.
The deterministic mid is what guarantees lex order; the jitter widens
the keyspace within the slot to defend against concurrent-insert
collisions on the same `(prev, next)` view.

Pass `random` to inject a deterministic source for tests; omit for
production (uses `Math.random`). The callback must honor the standard
`[0, 1)` contract; `jitter_suffix` clamps defensively for safety. The
jitter is best-effort collision avoidance, not a security primitive —
the server's `cell_item_position_taken` error is the load-bearing
safety net.
declarations.typeSignature: (a: string | null, b: string | null, random?: () => number): string

throws.description: if the bracket is invalid (`a >= b`), a bound breaks the

declarations.name: fractional_indices_between
declarations.docComment:
Generate `n` strictly-ordered keys between `a` and `b`.

Used for batch inserts (paste, bulk-import) so positions don't all
collapse onto a single deterministic mid-point. Output is monotonically
increasing; equal spacing is not guaranteed (clumping under the same
mid is acceptable and rare in practice).

Implemented by recursive bisection: pick the deterministic mid of
`(a, b)`, generate `floor(n/2)` keys in `(a, mid)`, the mid itself,
and the rest in `(mid, b)`. Each emitted key carries its own jitter.

Atomic on failure: if the bracket is structurally too tight at any
recursion depth, the whole call throws and no partial array is
returned. (Sub-brackets generated by `mid_between` never have the
tight shape, so this only fires when the *initial* bracket is tight.)
declarations.typeSignature: (a: string | null, b: string | null, n: number, random?: () => number): string[]

throws.description: if `n` is negative or non-integer, or if the initial bracket

modules.moduleComment:
Fractional indexing — generate ordered keys that admit insertion between
any two existing positions without rebalancing.

Used by `cell_item.position` to give parent → child membership a stable,
lex-sortable order. Two clients editing the same parent each compute keys
locally; the server applies them with `(parent_id, position)` as the
primary key. Concurrent inserts that hit the same key surface as a
`cell_item_position_taken` error; helper-side jitter (a small random
suffix on every generated key) keeps the collision probability negligible
at realistic UX concurrency.

**Alphabet**: base62 (`0`-`9`, `A`-`Z`, `a`-`z`). `'0'` is the least
digit; `'z'` the greatest. Keys are read as base62 fractions: lex order
over keys equals numeric order over fractions.

**Invariants emitted by this generator**:
- No emitted key ends in `'0'`. Without this rule, `key + '0'` would
  lex-equal `key` for ordering purposes, losing the room-to-insert that
  the trailing-`'0'` extension would otherwise provide.
- Every emitted key is non-empty and matches `FRACTIONAL_INDEX_REGEX`.
- Every emitted key is `≤ FRACTIONAL_INDEX_LENGTH_MAX`. The cap is checked
  on both inputs and output: bounds within a few digits of the cap can
  push a generated key over it, and the generator throws in that case
  rather than emitting an oversized key the wire would reject. In
  realistic use keys stay far under 100 chars, so this never fires.

**Wire vs. emitted invariants**: `FRACTIONAL_INDEX_REGEX` enforces the
alphabet + non-empty contract for any client-supplied position. The
no-trailing-`'0'` rule is stricter and lives only in this generator;
the wire admits the looser form so a future encoder can sit alongside
this one without a wire-schema bump.

**MVP scope — unsupported bracket shapes**:
- Unbounded prepend below `'0…'`: a long sequence of front-inserts
  eventually requires a key lex-less than every `'0'`-only key.
- Brackets shaped `(a, a + '0…0')` for any non-empty zero tail: the
  no-trailing-`'0'` invariant means no valid key fits the gap.

Both surface as explicit throws. Realistic UX bounds keep emitted
keys well under 100 chars after thousands of consecutive front- or
back-inserts; consumers that genuinely need the prepend extension
should request a negative-prefix scheme.

declarations.name: FsError
declarations.docComment:
Discriminated filesystem error kinds.

The shared error shape for `Result<{value: T}, FsError>`-returning filesystem
deps across the ecosystem. Callers branch on `kind` instead of regex-matching
`message`. The set is deliberately small — add kinds only when a caller
needs to distinguish them.

declarations.name: FsJsonError
declarations.docComment:
Extends `FsError` with `invalid_json` for `read_json`-style ops where the
file exists but parse fails. Callers can distinguish missing from corrupt
(e.g. self-healing config loads) without regex-matching `message`.

declarations.name: fs_classify_error
declarations.docComment:
Classifies a thrown filesystem error into a discriminated `FsError`.

Reads the Node `code` property (`ENOENT`/`EACCES`/`EPERM`/`EEXIST`) — Deno
surfaces the same codes when throwing from `node:fs/promises`. Unknown codes
fall through to `io_error`.

declarations.name: fs_exists
declarations.docComment: Checks if a file or directory exists.

declarations.name: fs_empty_dir
declarations.docComment: Empties a directory, recursively by default. If `should_remove` is provided, only entries where it returns `true` are removed.
declarations.typeSignature: (dir: string, should_remove?: ((name: string) => boolean) | undefined, options?: RmOptions | undefined): Promise<void>

members.name: filter
members.docComment: One or more filter functions, any of which can short-circuit the search by returning `false`.

members.name: file_filter
members.docComment: One or more file filter functions. Every filter must pass for a file to be included.

members.name: sort
members.docComment: Pass `null` or `false` to speed things up at the cost of volatile ordering.
members.typeSignature: boolean | null | ((a: ResolvedPath, b: ResolvedPath) => number)

members.name: include_directories
members.docComment: Set to `true` to include directories. Defaults to `false`.

members.name: cwd
members.docComment: Sets the cwd for `dir` unless it's an absolute path or `null`.

declarations.name: noop
declarations.docComment: Does nothing when called.

declarations.name: noop_async
declarations.docComment: Async function that returns a resolved promise.

declarations.name: resolved
declarations.docComment: A singleton resolved `Promise`.

declarations.name: identity
declarations.docComment: Returns the first arg.

declarations.name: Thunk
declarations.docComment:
Represents a value wrapped in a function.
Useful for lazy values and bridging reactive boundaries in Svelte.

declarations.name: unthunk
declarations.docComment:
Returns the result of calling `value` if it's a function,
otherwise it's like the `identity` function and passes it through.

declarations.name: GitInfo
declarations.docComment: Basic git repository info.

declarations.name: git_info_get
declarations.docComment:
Get basic git info (commit hash and branch name) without throwing.
Returns null values if git commands fail (e.g., not in a git repo).

declarations.name: git_current_branch_name
declarations.docComment: Returns the current git branch name.

throws.description: if the underlying git command fails

declarations.name: git_remote_branch_exists
declarations.docComment: Checks if a remote git branch exists.
declarations.typeSignature: (origin?: GitOrigin, branch?: GitBranch | undefined, options?: SpawnOptions | undefined): Promise<boolean>

throws.description: if the `git ls-remote` command fails for a reason other than the branch not existing

declarations.name: git_local_branch_exists
declarations.docComment: Checks if a local git branch exists.

declarations.name: GitWorkspaceStatus
declarations.docComment: Git workspace status flags indicating which types of changes are present.

declarations.name: git_parse_workspace_status
declarations.docComment:
Parses the output of `git status --porcelain -z` (v1 format) into a status object.
This is a pure function that can be tested independently.

Format: XY path\0 where:
- X = staged status (index)
- Y = unstaged status (work tree)
- path = file path (unescaped with -z)

Supported status codes:
- M = modified
- A = added
- D = deleted
- R = renamed
- C = copied
- T = type changed (regular file, symbolic link or submodule)
- U = unmerged
- ? = untracked
- ! = ignored

For renames/copies: XY new\0old\0 (two NUL-separated paths)

Note: This implementation treats submodules the same as regular files.
Submodule-specific status codes (lowercase m, ?) are interpreted as changes.

parameters.name: stdout
parameters.description: the raw output from `git status --porcelain -z`

declarations.name: git_check_workspace
declarations.docComment:
Checks the git workspace status using a single `git status --porcelain -z` call.
The -z format provides more reliable parsing by using NUL separators and avoiding escaping.

declarations.name: git_workspace_is_clean
declarations.returnDescription: `true` if the workspace has no changes at all

declarations.name: git_workspace_is_fully_staged
declarations.returnDescription: `true` if the workspace has no unstaged changes and no untracked files (staged changes are OK)

declarations.name: git_workspace_status_message
declarations.docComment: Converts a workspace status to a human-readable message.

declarations.name: git_check_clean_workspace
declarations.returnDescription: an error message if the git workspace has any unstaged or uncommitted changes, or `null` if it's clean

declarations.name: git_check_fully_staged_workspace
declarations.returnDescription: an error message if the git workspace has any unstaged changes or untracked files, or `null` if fully staged

throws.description: if the underlying git command fails

declarations.name: blake3_ready
declarations.docComment:
Resolves when the BLAKE3 WASM module is initialized and ready.
Initialization starts eagerly on import. Idempotent — safe to await multiple times.
In Node.js/Deno (sync init), this resolves immediately.

declarations.name: hash_blake3
declarations.docComment: Computes a BLAKE3 hash synchronously.

parameters.name: data
parameters.description: String or binary data to hash. Strings are UTF-8 encoded.

declarations.returnDescription: 64-character hexadecimal hash string (32 bytes)

declarations.name: Blake3Hash
declarations.docComment: Zod schema for a BLAKE3 hex hash — 64 lowercase hex characters (256-bit output).

modules.moduleComment:
BLAKE3 cryptographic hashing via `@fuzdev/blake3_wasm`.

Synchronous and fast. Returns hex-encoded 256-bit (32-byte) digests.
WASM initialization starts eagerly on import. Await `blake3_ready` before first use
in browser contexts where the WASM must be fetched asynchronously.

declarations.name: hash_sha1
declarations.docComment: Computes a SHA-1 hash using Web Crypto API.

parameters.name: data
parameters.description: String or binary data to hash. Strings are UTF-8 encoded.

declarations.returnDescription: 40-character hexadecimal hash string

declarations.name: hash_sha256
declarations.docComment: Computes a SHA-256 hash using Web Crypto API.

parameters.name: data
parameters.description: String or binary data to hash. Strings are UTF-8 encoded.

declarations.returnDescription: 64-character hexadecimal hash string

declarations.name: hash_sha384
declarations.docComment: Computes a SHA-384 hash using Web Crypto API.

parameters.name: data
parameters.description: String or binary data to hash. Strings are UTF-8 encoded.

declarations.returnDescription: 96-character hexadecimal hash string

declarations.name: hash_sha512
declarations.docComment: Computes a SHA-512 hash using Web Crypto API.

parameters.name: data
parameters.description: String or binary data to hash. Strings are UTF-8 encoded.

declarations.returnDescription: 128-character hexadecimal hash string

declarations.name: hash_insecure
declarations.docComment:
Computes a fast non-cryptographic hash using DJB2 algorithm.
Use for content comparison and cache keys, not security.

Note: Strings use UTF-16 code units, buffers use raw bytes.
For non-ASCII, `hash_insecure(str) !== hash_insecure(encoder.encode(str))`.

parameters.name: data
parameters.description: string or binary data to hash

declarations.returnDescription: 8-character hex-encoded unsigned 32-bit hash

modules.moduleComment:
Hash utilities using Web Crypto API and DJB2.

Provides `hash_sha256`, `hash_sha384`, `hash_sha512`, `hash_sha1` (Web Crypto, async)
and `hash_insecure` (DJB2, fast non-cryptographic).
For BLAKE3, see `hash_blake3` in `hash_blake3.ts`.

declarations.name: to_hex
declarations.docComment: Converts a `Uint8Array` to a lowercase hex string.
declarations.returnDescription: hex string with two characters per byte

declarations.name: from_hex
declarations.docComment:
Decodes a hex string to a `Uint8Array`.
Whitespace is stripped before parsing. Returns `null` for invalid hex.

parameters.name: hex
parameters.description: hex string to decode (case-insensitive, whitespace allowed)

declarations.returnDescription: decoded bytes, or `null` if the input is not valid hex

modules.moduleComment: Hex encoding and decoding helpers.

declarations.name: is_uuid
declarations.docComment: Loosely validates a UUID string.

declarations.name: UUID_MATCHER
declarations.docComment:
Postgres doesn't support the namespace prefix, so neither does Felt.
For more see the UUID RFC - https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc4122
The Ajv validator does support the namespace, hence this custom implementation.

declarations.name: create_client_id_creator
declarations.docComment: Creates a string id generator function, outputting `${name}_${count}` by default.
declarations.typeSignature: (name: string, count?: number | undefined, separator?: string): ClientIdCreator

declarations.name: count_iterator
declarations.docComment: Useful for fast counting when `.length` is not available.

declarations.name: JsonType
declarations.docComment: Like `typeof json`, but includes arrays. Excludes `'undefined'` because it's not valid JSON.

declarations.name: json_type_of
declarations.docComment:
Returns the `JsonType` of `value`, which is like `typeof json`
but includes `'array'` and omits `'undefined'`.

declarations.name: json_embed
declarations.docComment:
Embeds `data` as a JSON string, escaping single quotes.
Useful for optimizing JSON in JS because it parses faster.

declarations.name: json_stringify_deterministic
declarations.docComment:
Serializes a value to JSON with deterministic key ordering.
Recursively sorts object keys alphabetically for consistent hashing.
declarations.returnDescription: deterministic JSON string representation

declarations.name: SourceJson
declarations.docComment:
A library's analyzed source: the module metadata produced by `svelte-docinfo`.

`modules` uses `svelte-docinfo`'s wire shape (`ModuleJsonInput`), the same
shape its Vite plugin's `virtual:svelte-docinfo` and CLI emit. `svelte-docinfo`
is an optional peer dependency — install it to type this field, otherwise the
reference degrades to `any`.

declarations.name: LibraryJson
declarations.docComment:
A library, as two clean projections of its inputs: `pkg_json` (the curated,
publish-safe subset of `package.json`) and `source_json` (the `svelte-docinfo`
analysis). Both are raw data — every derived value (repo url, npm url,
`published`, the module/declaration hierarchy, …) is computed by the consumer
(fuz_ui's `Library` class), not stored here, so nothing can go stale.

declarations.name: library_json_from_modules
declarations.docComment:
Builds a `LibraryJson` from a `package.json` and analyzed `modules`.

Curates `package_json` to the publish-safe `PkgJson` subset via
`pkg_json_from_package_json`, so a full `package.json` (the common case from
gro's loader or a JSON import) is accepted and stripped, while an
already-curated `PkgJson` passes through unchanged.

`keys` overrides the curated field set, and must match the `keys` passed to
the build-time strip (fuz_ui's `vite_plugin_pkg_json`): the re-strip here
drops any field not in `keys`, so a wider build-time list with the default
here would silently discard the extras. Pass the same shared list to both.
